title: Anomalous spin-pumping behavior of half-metallic ferromagnet/d-wave superconductor heterostructures
authors: Hadi H. Hassan, Santiago J. Carreira, M. Cabero, F. Martinet, Alexander Buzdin, Jacobo Santamaria, Javier E. Villegas
abstract: Spin-pumping experiments in superconductor/ferromagnet heterostructures, which probe spin-sinking by the superconductor, have revealed a variety of complex behaviors. Most studies have focused on conventional s-wave superconductors combined with metallic or insulating ferromagnets. Here, we study a d-wave superconductor paired with a half-metallic ferromagnet, in epitaxial YBa2Cu3O7-d/La0.7Sr0.3MnO3 heterostructures with two crystalline orientations: one in which YBCO is c-axis oriented, and the other in which YBCO grows along the (103) direction. Using ferromagnetic resonance (FMR), we probe the temperature-dependent Gilbert damping coefficient α. For (103) heterostructures, α(T) initially decreases below Tc, but then increases at lower temperatures, exceeding normal-state levels. This behavior can be understood in terms of the opening of the superconducting gap and spin transport via nodal quasiparticles, which dominate when the ab-plane of YBCO is exposed at the interface. In stark contrast, c-axis heterostructures exhibit a pronounced enhancement of α(T) below Tc, peaking at 0.65-0.7Tc before decaying. This anomaly suggests the dominance of interface-bound Andreev states, arising from a locally suppressed superconducting order parameter due to proximity effects with the half-metallic LSMO.
summary: This paper explores how the orientation of a d-wave superconductor interface affects spin transport from a half-metallic ferromagnet. It reveals that while one orientation allows transport via nodal quasiparticles, the other creates interface-bound Andreev states that cause an unexpected increase in magnetic damping below the superconducting transition.
main_problem: Investigating the anomalous temperature-dependent spin-pumping behavior and spin-transport mechanisms in heterostructures composed of a d-wave superconductor and a half-metallic ferromagnet.
main_result: The study finds that crystalline orientation dictates the spin-sinking mechanism: (103) heterostructures show damping increases due to nodal quasiparticles, while c-axis heterostructures exhibit an anomalous damping peak due to interface-bound Andreev states.
method: Broadband ferromagnetic resonance (FMR) was used to measure the temperature-dependent Gilbert damping coefficient in epitaxial YBCO/LSMO bilayers.
model_or_system: Epitaxial bilayers of the d-wave superconductor YBa2Cu3O7-d (YBCO) and the half-metallic ferromagnet La0.7Sr0.3MnO3 (LSMO) grown on NdGaO3 substrates in both c-axis and (103) orientations.
key_observables: Gilbert damping coefficient (alpha), FMR linewidth, magnetization (M), and superconducting transition temperature (Tc).
important_parameters: Temperature relative to Tc, crystalline orientation (c-axis vs 103), and the presence of nodal vs thermal quasiparticles.
main_assumptions: The behavior is driven by the proximity effect and the specific geometry of the d-wave superconducting gap at the interface.
figures_summary: Figure 1 includes a schematic of the coplanar waveguide setup and a diagram of the LSMO/YBCO bilayer illustrating magnetization precession and spin pumping.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of spin-pumping in d-wave/half-metal interfaces, describes the epitaxial growth and structural characterization of the heterostructures, presents FMR results showing orientation-dependent damping, and concludes with a physical interpretation based on quasiparticle and Andreev state dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig 1 : (a) sketch of the coplanar waveguide and the sample mounted on top, where the DC and rf fields directions are indicated. (b) sketch of the LSMO/YBCO bilayer, where the processing magnetization in the LSMO yield to spin pumping and a spin current into YBCO.

FIG. 2. (a-c) X-ray diffraction (XRD) of a LSMO-YBCO bilayer grown on (a) NGO (110) and (c) NGO (100). In (b) and (d) we show in detail the Bragg peaks of LSMO and YBCO around the (b) NGO (220) and (d) NGO (200). The linear trends in the insets in (b) and (d) were calculated from the maxima and minima of the Laue oscillations around the LSMO peak. The XRD measurement shows epitaxial growth of the bilayer along the YBCO c-axis, while the calculated Laue thicknesses are in agreement with the nominal LSMO thickness.

Figure 3 (a) Cross sectional HAADF-STEM image of an LSMO/YBCO interface. (b) Higher magnification view of the yellow boxed region in (a), with the red/white arrows indicating the red/black intensity profiles of the cationic columns shown below (red for APB plane, black for the one below it). Scale bars: (a) 2 nm and (b) 1 nm. (c) Cross sectional HAADF-STEM images of an LSMO/YBCO sample grown on a NGO [110] substrate. Upper panel reveals the substrate-film interfaces and surface roughness of the YBCO layer. The lower panel displays an atomic resolution image of the atomically sharp LSMO/YBCO interface.
